text: My name is Daniel Murariu of Allegheny General Hospital and I'd like to describe a novel robotically harvested vertical rectus abdominis myocutaneous flap following robotic abdominal perineal resection. I'd like to thank Dr. Richard Fortunato for the colorectal portion.

We have a fifty-two year old male with BMI of thirty-three with a distal locally advanced rectal cancer who had undergone a neoadjuvant with chemoradiation. This marks the skin paddle and the proposed X is the Doppler bowl vessel. The patient underwent an extrasynthetic perineal resection due to the size and location of the tumor.

The surgeon places two additional ports on the left side to triangulate the right rectus muscle for the robotically harvested vertical rectus abdominis myocutaneous (VRAM) flap. An assistant palpates the abdomen, allowing the surgeon to mark the superior aspect of the dissection with electrocautery.
The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath to expose the rectus abdominis muscle medially. The muscle is then dissected out, extending both superiorly and inferiorly.
The robotic system is used to perform a straightforward dissection, aided by insufflation for enhanced visualization. The muscle flap is dissected laterally.

The surgeon harvests and dissects the pedicle of the deep inferior epigastric vessels to facilitate the rotation of the flap into the pelvic defect. The rectus abdominis muscle is cut at its origin from the pubic crest using a ligature device, marking the intra-abdominal elevation component of the procedure.
The surgeon scrubs in and incises the skin paddle, then elevates the muscle superiorly under direct visualization, completing the elevation of the muscle flap.

Indocyanine Green is injected, demonstrating good perfusion of the skin paddle. The skin paddle is then placed into the abdominal cavity and passed into the pelvic defect, where it is sutured into position.
